data.title: Is This The Golden Age Of Macro Investing?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Nicholas Bohnsack, President and CEO of Strategas Securities, and Amy Zhang, Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager at Alger, reveal the powerful currents driving markets in 2025.

Transcript

Mike Larson: Macroanalysis is as important as it's ever been, given key questions about the Fed, the economy, and big picture themes like the AI boom. That's the message I heard from one of my MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast guests this week. From the other, that bottoms up research, the identification of corporate inflection points, and the ability to let your winners run when you get the investing story right is most essential. And that's why I'm so excited about this week's episode.

You're going to get two different world views from two different experts who specialize in two different approaches to the market, and they're both all new guests on the podcast. One is Nicholas Bohnsack, President and CEO at Strategas Securities, and the other is Amy Zhang, Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager at Alger. Thank you both for joining me.

Amy, Nicholas, or I guess NB is what you said to use for you. It's great that I could have you on the show here. I appreciate you both taking some time out for the podcast. Since you're both first-time guests, I would appreciate if you maybe just introduce yourself a little bit, talk about your background in the business, and what it is that you do at each of your firms. Nicholas, I guess we'll start with you at Strategas, and then we'll roll over to Amy.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Okay, very nice. Well, it's certainly nice to be here and with you both. Yeah. So I'm Nicholas Bohnsack. I'm the President of Strategas Securities or Strategas Asset Management. We are a macro research and asset management house, focused on all the major macro verticals and ultimately our clients. We endeavor to be the one-stop shop for macro. So our clients are institutional investors, wealth advisors, and individuals alike.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. And, Amy, same to you from Alger.

Amy Zhang: Yeah. I'm the Executive Vice President and Portfolio Manager for our small- and mid-cap products at Alger. I run four products - Small-Cap Focus, Small-Cap Growth, Mid-Cap Focus, and ETF (FRTY) which just surpassed $100 million. Alger is -- I consider Alger a growth pioneer, and we've been in business for over 60 years.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. Well, there's a lot to unpack. We'll probably start a little bit at macro and then go from there. So Nicholas, I'm going to go to you first. In this environment, obviously, we're sort of hovering around highs. We've had an incredible rally back from all the tariff- and trade-induced sell-off experience we had in February, March, and April. What is your take on where markets stand? I mean, how would you characterize this environment?

Nicholas Bohnsack: Yeah. It's funny. We go through a pretty, I think, exhaustive only because we do it all the time, exercise trying to poke holes in whatever the conventional thesis is. And truth be told, it's very difficult to find a lot to be overly concerned about with respect to risk assets. And so when you consider sort of that as a starting point, it's tough to fade the bull market in equities right now. It's perhaps tough to fade, even some of the bull market that exists in some tranches of credit. But at the same time, valuations are getting to fairly lofty levels.

Corporate credit spreads are on 25-, 27-, 28-year tights. We clearly have a lot of uncertainty as it relates to policy and the implementation of policy, even if we have some clarification on taxes and tariffs. So the way I look at it increasingly is that the market is pretty priced to perfection. May go a little bit further, but we want to start to train our attentions towards what could go wrong. And there may be a little bit of chop and volatility here as we move towards the year-end.

Mike Larson: Got it. Amy, what about your thoughts on sort of the big picture before we kind of dive into the small- and mid-cap space? I mean, do you tend to align with what NB was saying there, or are you a little more constructive, less so?

Amy Zhang: Well, I'm very constructive on small- and mid-cap growth companies because I agree with Nick that in terms of -- market has been very, very narrow, right? So the big mega-cap -- the Magnificent Seven and the mega-cap has been done so well. And so valuation, I think, is very high. And the valuation gap between large-cap and mid-cap growth companies, I think, is so wide that a truck can drive through it, right?

So historically, usually trading -- you would trade as a premium, given the law of small numbers for small- and mid-cap growth companies. And we still have a lot of high quality small- and mid-cap companies. So to that extent, I think, now I'm very excited. I think finally, we're done with the rate hikes, right? Part of the reason why small -- especially small-cap growth has been the worst performing asset class for the last three years. I've been in business for 30 years, and I think that was the worst three years.

So, finally, I think the headwinds of rate hikes are going to turn into tailwinds of rate cuts. So that itself is going to be a very positive factor in a small- and mid-cap growth. But, of course, it's also fundamentals, with which we focus a lot on. I'm very excited because I think finally, with headwinds turning into tailwinds, we will finally get back to stock pickers market for small- and mid-cap growth. The market is going to continue to broaden.

Mike Larson: I'm glad you brought that up because we had that day or those couple of days after, I think, the CPI numbers where the Russell had a pretty big move to the upside. And that was one of the things some of the strategists said, "Oh, we've been lagging." But we see that part of the market kick-in, and it seems to -- you seem to think this is probably sustainable. Yes?

Amy Zhang: Yes. Because I think labor market probably will continue to be weak because AI is very deflationary, right? Like, so to that extent, that's a transformative force that I think it just and -- economy, hopefully, we can muddle through, but we are worried about maybe the growth.

So growth is going to become scarce. So, what we do is identify exceptional small- and mid-cap companies that have the wherewithal to become exceptional large companies. So when growth become scarce, that kind of company can truly grow through idiosyncratic drivers will really shine. So I think it's going to -- the winner is going to shine through, and that's our job at Alger to be stock pickers.

Mike Larson: Got it. Nicholas, let me pivot back to you and talk about the big picture. I mean, we've got -- we're sort of parsing Powell this week depending what he has to say. We're all wondering CPI versus PPI, what's right, what's leading, and all that. What are your thoughts on that being a market driver one way or the other? I mean, do you think we're overly concerned about the Fed here, under concerned?

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, we certainly spend a lot of time wringing our hands over what they'll do next. And I'd say political pressure notwithstanding, the Fed is trying to really articulate the last 20 yards of a pretty difficult track, right? You've got inflation, as you mentioned, in some of the PCE and other producer price data that remains pretty sticky. And I think there's a fair argument to be made that some of the tariff-related price adjustments, albeit one time, could lend themselves to sort of re-elevating inflation as a principal point of concern.

So the Fed has a sort of a tricky dance to play. I also think though that the case is building for monetary policy to be more neutral as opposed to restrictive. And that may be what the president is getting at. Of course, the proclivity of most presidents would be for easier monetary policy outside sort of the exogenous circumstances. But I don't know that we're spending too much time on it.

At the end of the day, monetary policy probably has to move back into neutral territory. How quickly they do that is what will provide some dislocation. If you think of the consensus believing that a 25-basis point cut is going to come in September or even as the Treasury secretary interestingly suggested a 50-basis point cut, you look at the two-year, they want 75 basis points of cuts. So there's a fairly wide gap between where the market is, where the prognosticators are, and maybe even where the administration is.

What I look at as we move towards the end of the year is, can the sort of organic drivers of growth - we talk about artificial intelligence, we talk about the industrial power renaissance, we talk about some of the other CapEx incentivized investments that might be made up on the back of the tax bill and the spending bill - can those kick-in before we need accommodative policy to prop the economy up?

Our eyes will really be focused on labor, and I think, the Fed comes back into the picture when you start talking about labor. So I'm not sure that we're spending too much time. It's really a function of how long it's going to take for the Fed to get where it needs to be.

Mike Larson: Got it. Well, let's talk about the earnings outlook here. I mean, you know, we're pretty much through the second quarter earnings season. I think I saw the number. We're up about 11% and heading into season, we're only about expectations were about 4% growth. What are you seeing in the big picture, Nicholas? And I guess, Amy, then I would ask you in the small- and mid-cap space what kind of growth you're seeing.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, I'll start with the earnings picture in general. I think two major takeaways from the second quarter. Number one is, of course, we had the post-Liberation Day downward revision, lowering of expectations. You note here in the question that we've exceeded those. Importantly, we've exceeded the levels that existed prior to Liberation Day. So all in a very, very strong quarter in the aggregate. I think the takeaway though is that the mix is changing. So whereas you see strength in tech, strength in financials, you're seeing some weakness in industrials, some weakness in healthcare. So the deck chairs are being rearranged for the second half of the year, and that's why I think those special drivers are going to be really, really important.

The other point that I would make, which has really been key to our, I guess, embrace of the bullish thesis generally is, can companies continue to build the revenue stack? If you can get top-line growth irrespective of how you might manage to the bottom-line, this economy is in really good shape. If that starts to falter, then we may have a little bit of trouble ahead, particularly if inflation remains stickier towards the back end of this year and early next year.

Mike Larson: And, Amy, again, in your space, when you're listening to these conference calls and you're hearing what some of the companies had to say, what's the general story out of the small- and mid-cap space for the earnings season here?

Amy Zhang: Well, we had overall very strong earnings season. And I just want to point out, I think Nicholas is -- when Nick's going to talk about revenue, I think it's very important for us. It's our -- my playbook is really about identifying companies early, right, and the law of small numbers. So we define smallness in terms of revenue when we -- especially for small-cap. So to that extent, it's all about -- for us, it's really all about robust and sustainable, durable revenue growth.

So with that, operating leverage goes both ways, right? So you -- once you have that and then you of course, we focus on company with strong unit economics, then you would trickle down to the bottom-line. So it's really about companies who can grow, have robust top-line growth and also, have margin expansion and a profitability with your EPS growth, and EPS will drive stock price. I think that will be -- because for us, valuation is already so low, right? So it wasn't being compressed. So the companies can truly grow top-line and bottom-line will continue to shine. And those are the kind of companies we focus on.

Mike Larson: Any particular sectors within your space? Or if you're comfortable naming a couple of names, stocks that you think really have a good story to tell here?

Amy Zhang: Yeah. At Alger, we're bottom-up investors, but we're not like looking at companies at vacuum. We really care about the key themes, and we're benchmark agnostic as well. But as a whole, clearly, we've been, for example, overweight industrials. Clearly, with AI, the common sort of theme we are in are data center and infrastructure and so forth. But we also invest in compounders, right, in industrial, which, I can maybe name one, like RBC Bearings (RBC), right?

There's a company that does highly engineering precision bearings. They have mission-critical products like airplanes, submarines, and missile defense systems. So, they have three segments. Aerospace and defense has been fast growing but very strong. Industrial, they have -- their industrial space is very sort of diversified, not cyclical. And, actually, the big and beautiful bill will benefit them. So, to that extent, the company has been generating massive cash flows with strong top-line growth. And, we -- this is a company we invested in November 2020. We still believe that they can continue to be high quality compounder.

So, that's actually an industrial name that maybe not every -- not necessarily a household name. But we really invest in every sector -- it's really about finding the hidden gem. And small- and mid-cap is all about the discovery value. So, one of the -- I know small-cap -- not only small-cap growth has been the worst asset class, but small-cap health has been the worst sector. But even with that, I think we always believe, I always believe, we always invest in companies that we want to save time, life, money, headaches. I think healthcare is still very important.

So, one of the companies that we invested in about a year ago, September last year, is called GeneDx (WGS). GeneDx is a genetic testing company that focus on exome and genome testing for rare disease. So they started with children, pediatrician and pediatrics. And then they -- going forward, we think they can expand. They're already expanding to babies and going forward will be adults. But one out of the ten Americans have rare disease. Half of them are children. So clearly, what they do is very important.

And, specifically, recently, this year June, just a several -- a few months ago, the American Academy of Pediatrics issued updated guidelines that elevated their testing to be first tier test for children with global developmental delay or intellectual disability. So that was a huge validation, even though they're not going to start generating revenue from that specific catalyst until next year. But so far, the company has generated tremendous revenue and profitability growth. We always look for the inflection point.

So when we invest in it, the quarter we invested in September, that quarter, they become profitable. So, for us -- and then they are very small. Their revenue last -- last 12 months revenue is only about $360 million, and their market future TAM could be over $40 billion. So clearly, law of small numbers, and they're really hitting on all cylinders. So, I think still even, again, like, in every sector, there's very interesting players. And, last but not least, they are also very underappreciated AI play because they have a very strong moat.

In genetics testing, data is super important. And especially the interpretation of data is the heart of the whole business that they can do it faster, cheaper, with more accuracy than their competitors, which gives them a very wide moat. And so this is really a very much underappreciated AI play.

And last but not least, we also have a long-term holding in Wingstop (WING). That's a consumer name that you're probably familiar with, highly -- very high-quality company that in a -- I think they're really in the category of one in terms of unit growth and comp, but also highest industry-leading cash on cash return, 70%. But more importantly, I would say, Wingstop is really a technology company that sell chicken wings.

Most recently, they roll out Smart Kitchen, right, with AI engines to predict demands, and they have other -- they use AI in other areas. So, that can cut into, like, say, wait time in half just with the Smart Kitchen. So, anyway, we really focus on scalable, profitable, growth companies.

Mike Larson: Well, my daughter is a fan of Wingstop. So, when she watches this, she'll be happy to hear that recommendation. Nicholas, I guess I would -- same question to you, whether I don't know if you can talk individual names or just sectors. Things that you think look potentially more attractive heading into the rest of the back half of this year, just given sort of your macro view.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Yeah. I mean, for us, macro really comes down to themes. And as you know, we manage a small suite of three exchange-traded funds, and the one that I'm responsible to principally is the Strategas Macro Thematic Opportunities funds, $200 million now SAMT. And what's, I think, important about it is that it's thematic rotation. And so it's not beholden to any one theme at any one time. We can, as the business cycle evolves and the macro-environment evolves, we can rotate among the themes.

And so we have four principally right now that we're invested in. We mentioned artificial intelligence earlier. I do think there's a shift afoot from compute capacity building and large language model development to a more agentic, contextual inference sort of application of AI. That business use case is very, very appealing for some differentiation in the portfolio. We mentioned the industrial power renaissance due to this insatiable need for power, not just by Americans but globally. I mean, the number two use of power in this country is keeping things cold that want to be cold and keeping things hot that want to be hot, and that's not likely to abate. So, the industrial power renaissance is very much in the fore of our focus as we move to the balance of this year and into next.

And then, the two very quickly that we didn't mention earlier, number one is deglobalization. Fortunately or not, I think it's going to define a lot of the investment decision-making that we undertake over the next decade as a group. You saw it on full display yesterday at the White House and Alaska earlier this week.

So, deglobalization is principle, and it cuts through a lot of different threats. If it's intellectual property sharing, defense and rearmament, natural resource utilization, supply chains, et cetera, that's certainly of importance. And then the last one, which I think is a little bit of a -- I'm careful to use the word hedge, but we want to be mindful that things have gotten very rich, is cash flow aristocrats. These are companies that can self-fund in periods of uncertainty, whether that be uncertainty specific to their business or more broadly in the economy. So, from our vantage point, those are the four themes to watch as we move through the balance of this year and into early next.

Mike Larson: I guess sticking with you, Nicholas, I'm just wondering, are there any issues that you think maybe people aren't appreciating enough as a risk factor in this market? I mean, again, we've come -- we've had such a head of steam, and I'm just curious, I don't try and be a Debbie Downer or anything like that, but I'm kind of curious as we head into the back half here if there's something that you think people need to be kind of paying extra attention to.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Well, I'll -- listen, I'll be honest with you, man. When things are really bad, my job and our firm's job is to look for things that are great. And when things are really great, our job is to find the things that may be more worrisome. And so things are pretty good right now. I think the biggest concern I have, as you think about the underpinnings of the economy is what's happening in the labor market. There is a school of thought that says, hey, listen, some of the policies that have been undertaken by the administration or some of the efficiencies that have been perhaps gained by technology are causing some dislocation at the lower income cohorts, and that's absolutely true and valid.

But, on a propensity to spend basis, that's a far smaller group than what we see existing at the higher income and wealth cohorts. So labor generally and consumption looks pretty strong. But if you start to think about some of the tightness in credit, if you start to think about some of the pressures that could be intended on household credit in particular, that could start to weigh on the consumer. And so if I had to say, what's one thing that really worries you, and we're always a tweet away from Armageddon, it seems, it would be on the consumer side in sort of a classical economic sense, as we move through the balance of the year.

Inflation doesn't help that either because it's a regressive tax, obviously. We've managed to keep it at bay because of lower energy prices. But if that should reverse for any particular reason, the consumer comes under more pressure.

Mike Larson: Got it. Amy, and both of you obviously are going to be joining us for the 2025 MoneyShow Traders Expo in Orlando this October. Since, you're a new -- first-time speaker, Amy, any thoughts on, somebody comes to your presentation there, what it is they're going to pick up, what it is they're going to learn? I know it's still a little ways out, though.

Amy Zhang: Well, I hope I'll have opportunity to talk about the new cycle for small- and mid-cap companies that because I think the current policy will favor that. It may take time with lower rates, lower taxes, stimulus, and big beautiful bill, but also deregulation, which will foster more M&A and a small company specifically will benefit from that. And also, just maybe sort of the kind of playbook we have in exceptional growth companies. Because, as you know, the true -- they're only -- it's rare to find those exceptional growth companies. So, our job is to find them early and be directionally correct and let winners run. So, it's really about slugging. And I hope I will have opportunity to talk about those exciting opportunities.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. And, Nicholas, I know -- again, we always say we get these topic titles pretty far in advance. Yours is navigating crosscurrents, policy markets, and themes in the age of Trump. Anything that we haven't covered that, again, somebody's going to be coming to this to see you speak, what they're going to take away from that?

Nicholas Bohnsack: The only thing that I know for sure is that the importance of macro on portfolio construction and trading is not going to abate in the next six weeks. So we are living in, depending on your perspective, the golden age of macro and particularly its importance for investors to consider. So, we're going to bring our latest thinking on it. The title encapsulates sort of the broader lens. We've talked a lot about sort of the pertinent issues today. Those won't abate. We'll probably add a few more onto the stack, but macro is as important as it's ever been, if not more so, and I look forward to chatting about it with all of our guests.

Mike Larson: Wonderful. Well, Nicholas, Amy, thank you both so much for your time, your insights here.

Nicholas Bohnsack: Thank you.

Amy Zhang: Thank you.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BHK: Await Better Interest Rate Conditions Before Entry
data.description:
Overview

BlackRock Core Bond Trust (NYSE:BHK) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide current income from a portfolio of diverse income producing securities. Income funds like BHK can be an efficient way to offset market uncertainty and volatility that may be experienced with traditional equities. Therefore, BHK is one of those funds that have a very specific use case and may not be the best match for most investors. The fund has an inception dating back to 2001 and has a management fee of 0.5%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BHK's share price has declined by roughly 16.4%. The fund has maintained a continuous downtrend for nearly twelve months straight and even failed to participate in the market rally over recent months. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the BHK still has a total loss of 8.77%. One of the most attractive aspects to BHK is the generous starting dividend yield of 9.3%. However, the recent weakness in earnings makes me concerned about the sustainability of distributions.

The fund currently trades at a discount to NAV valuation following the price decline. However, the fund's sensitivity to the interest rate environment makes me cautious about the possibility of further downside movements. There are also risks related to the fund's use of leverage and exposure to securities with below investment grade credit ratings, which increases exposure to defaults. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that BHK implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, BHK has total managed assets of $948.9M that are spread across a wide range of securities. Under normal market conditions, BHK aims to invest at least 75% of assets to bonds that are rated as investment grade. The remaining 25% of assets are typically allocated to securities that are below investment grade, which can increase the overall risk profile of the fund. In an effort to mitigate these risks, BHK utilizes different types of securities to limit concentration risk. The breakdown by security is as follows:

  • Investment Grade Credit: 29.3%
  • High Yield: 20.6%
  • US Government Related: 20.6%
  • Securitized Products: 19.3%
  • Agency Mortgages: 12.1%
  • Non-US Developed: 8.3%
  • Bank Loans: 2.2%
  • Emerging Markets: 1.9%
  • Other: 0.4%

Expanding on the level of diversity, BHK spreads its assets across a range of industries. As we can see below, the fund is primarily allocated to Treasuries at 32.82%. This is followed by other investments labeled as 'No Industry' and Banking, accounting for 26.80% and 11.87% of assets respectively. 'No Industry' can simply mean that these holdings cannot be classified under a traditional labeling guideline and may include assets like derivatives or collateralized loan obligations. However, we can see the fund is exposed to more traditional industries like Communications, Consumer Non-Cyclical, and Technology as well.

Looking at the top holdings, we can see that treasuries make up the largest slice of assets at 17.43%. This is followed by some other notable names such as Federal National Mortgage Association (OTCQB:FNMA), Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp (OTCQB:FMCC), Bank of America (BAC), and Morgan Stanley (MS) just to name a few. However, investors should remember that BHK is exposed to these holdings through income producing securities, rather than common shares. Therefore, investors should have a limited expectation for capital appreciation to occur.

Just to demonstrate the fund's lackluster capital appreciation, I pulled in a performance chart of BHK over the last ten years. As we can see below, BHK's share price has declined by nearly 26% over the last decade. When including distributions, the total return jumps up to 46.3% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate most of BHK's return to come from the distributions that it can provide.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since BHK operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets within. As a result, BHK now trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 3.72%. For reference, BHK has traded at an average discount to NAV of 0.44% over the last three-year period. In order to better visualize the current discount level, I added the red line to the graph below to show the discount level in relation to the ten year range. As we can see, BHK's discount now sits below its usual range since 2021.

Despite the larger discount than average, I do not believe that this is a good time to accumulate shares. I reviewed the most recent annual report for 2024 to get an idea of the fund's internal performance. Net investment income amounted to $0.57 per share, while BHK experienced net realized losses of $0.39 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have declined over the last few years. I suspect that the decline can be attributed to the shrinking operating spread as a result of higher interest rates.

However, the fund's net investment income doesn't cover the distribution on its own. Therefore, BHK has remained reliant on net realized gains to fuel operational growth. The only problem is that net realized gains rely on positive market momentum so that BHK can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. As you can imagine, it's difficult for a fund to capture positive net realized gains when market indexes are declining. Due to the market volatility and uncertainty over recent years, the fund's NAV continues to decline. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $16.45 per share
  • 2021: $15.47 per share
  • 2022: $10.89 per share
  • 2023: $11.02 per share
  • 2024: $10.30 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, BHK utilizes leverage as a way to amplify earnings and take advantage of attractive opportunities. The use of leverage currently amounts to 24.1% of assets, which is equivalent to $230.8M. I consider this to be an aggressive amount of leverage during this period of elevated interest rates. The higher the interest rate, the higher the cost of leverage for the fund. Therefore, BHK's operating spread between the cost of debt and the yield collected from securities shrinks, which is why the fund has experienced a decline in net investment income. Investors should also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards securities that don't perform as anticipated.

Looking at the chart below, we can see that BHK's share price has demonstrated an inverse relationship with the interest rate environment. When interest rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the pandemic, BHK's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021. Conversely, BHK has remained suppressed since 2022, when interest rates were aggressively hiked to its decade high.

Additionally, BHK maintains some exposure to borrowers that are rated below investment grade. For reference, borrowers with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers with less consistent cash flows or they may be more reliant on debt financing to fuel operational growth. These are borrowers that may be more vulnerable to defaulting on their loans. The breakdown by rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade: (AAA - BBB): 71.72%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 28.23%
  • Unrated: 0.95%

However, I do believe that future interest rates can serve as a growth catalyst. Lower rates would improve the fund's operating spread and provide a more favorable environment for underlying holdings to appreciate in value. Markets seem to be anticipating interest rate cuts to take place as early as September, since the odds of a rate cut have increased following the latest core CPI data.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0746 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 9.3%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.8952 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, BHK has demonstrated difficulty generating a sufficient level of earnings to support the distributions that it pays. Just for reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $1.96 per share
  • 2021: $0.07 per share
  • 2022: ($3.76) per share
  • 2023: $1.03 per share
  • 2024: $0.18 per share

As we can see, BHK only generated a sufficient level of earnings in two of the last five years. Therefore, I worry about the sustainability of the distributions going forward. In a scenario where interest rates are cut, net investment income may actually improve since the cost of debt will decrease. Lower rates would essentially improve the fund's operating spread, while also providing some relief to securities within the portfolio. As we can see below, BHK's payouts continuously shift based on underlying market conditions and the interest rate environment. These shifts are just the nature of funds that have exposure to income producing securities that are structured on a floating rate basis.

It's also worth noting that BHK's distributions are typically funded by net investment income. Therefore, distributions received from BHK are generally classified as ordinary dividend income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in BHK may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate BHK as a hold for now despite the fund trading at a discount to its NAV. The fund has struggled to consistently generate a sufficient level of net investment income as a result of the higher interest rate environment. The fund's use of leverage has caused its operating spread between the cost of debt and the yield collected from securities to shrink. I do believe that future interest rate cuts can serve as a growth catalyst, but I will remain observant on the sidelines until that happens.

data.title: Dropbox: Business Is Still Declining
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Dropbox (NASDAQ:DBX) was a sell rating because I believe the business may have entered a phase of structural decline. DBX's recent results did little to change my mind. While margins expanded meaningfully, this was achieved by cost cuts rather than growth, and revenue continued to fall. More importantly, paying users and ARPU both declined, reflecting clear demand weakness. With management doubling down on profitability at the expense of growth, I think DBX is structurally shrinking its core base, leaving little reason to expect a sustainable turnaround.

2Q25 results update

From the headlines, DBX's 2Q25 performance looked solid thanks to the better-than-expected profitability, but a deeper look tells a different story. For context, adj. EBIT margin expanded to 41.5%, up from 35.9% in 2Q24, and is well above the 37.5% guidance. This performance, however, was not driven by improved demand or anything like that, but by cost management. Specifically, DBX cut employee-related expenses with workforce reductions and also reduced marketing spend. Obviously, if you cut costs, margins would go up, but this doesn’t change the bearish view that I have—DBX is pulling back on growth investments rather than leaning in. This, in my view, overshadows the margin expansion story because it means that the topline is likely to continue shrinking. And indeed, revenue continues to decline, down 1.4% y/y to $625.7 million.

Adj. EPS Growth Masks Weak Fundamentals

Staying on the results discussion, I believe a key talking point is the 18% y/y growth in adj. EPS to $0.71. If you just look at this growth, you would think that it is a strong result, but in reality, I believe it masks the underlying weakness of the business. Adj. net income was essentially flat, growing only slightly from $194.1 million to $197.7 million. The real driver of EPS growth was not stronger fundamentals but aggressive share repurchases. In 2Q25, DBX spent ~$400 million buying back 14 million shares, which effectively reduced the diluted share count by ~15% y/y.

Of course, returning capital is not negative, but relying on buybacks to drive double-digit EPS growth in the face of revenue decline is a red flag to me. My belief is that this reflects a company that is increasingly leaning on its balance sheet to drive adj. EPS growth rather than organically (which seems unlikely since DBX is pulling back on growth investments).

In any case, my opinion is this approach does little to resolve my main concern—the business is unable to grow.

Core Business Continues to Shrink

I still don’t see any signs of recovery in DBX’s core business. The operating metrics continue to prove my bear thesis right. Paying users fell to 18.13 million in 2Q25, down 34,000 sequentially and from 18.22 million in 2Q24. This is about as clear as it can get that demand is falling. The more troubling aspect is management’s own outlook, which calls for a net loss of ~300,000 paying users in FY25. That guidance basically dashes any hope bullish investors may have for a near-term recovery. Importantly, this means that all the incremental product adjustments are not preventing users from leaving. A big part of this weakness is actually self-inflicted. Management acknowledged that the decision to pull back on the FormSwift business was a key reason for the sequential decline in paying users and also created a 140 bps headwind to revenue growth and a 160 bps drag on ARR.

Monetization trends tell a similar story of weakness. Average revenue per paying user [ARPU] fell to $138.32 from $139.93 in 2Q24. This decline reflects two self-inflicted pains. Firstly, the de-prioritization of FormSwift, which had carried higher average selling prices, and secondly, the continued rollout of the lower-priced “Simple” plan.

The narrative from management is that this is part of a broader effort to focus on profitability. But in reality, these actions of rolling back FormSwift and launching a cheaper plan simply accelerate the decline in both user counts and monetization. Notably, and arguably the most important bearish dynamic here is that by pulling back on FormSwift and reducing the outbound sales force that supported the core file sync and share [FSS] business, DBX is shrinking its addressable base.

All in all, these dynamics paint a very negative picture of DBX's outlook, as it is now faced with declining paying users, each spending less on average, and management is pursuing strategies that actively further accelerate this decline.

Dash Remains a Distant and Speculative Hope

Like I have said before, Dash is still in its early innings, and this has not changed, as there is still no concrete evidence of its potential to become a meaningful growth driver. Management continues to speak positively about early engagement signals, noting strong sequential growth in metrics like weekly active users. However, when it comes to the financial impact, there is just nothing to show. The longer it takes for Dash to show tangible P&L contributions, the value of this “call option” becomes lesser as the market would reset expectations. The next catalyst here for Dash is the launch of a self-serve version in the coming months. Should that also fail to drive widespread adoption and contribute to P&L, I just don’t see this as something that can credibly offset the decay in the core business.

Valuation

I don’t see any fundamental reasons to justify DBX trading any higher than where it is today—10xforward PE. In fact, I don’t think DBX even deserves to trade at 10x because the business is simply not growing. The thing supporting DBS adj. EPS growth now is its aggressive share buyback program, but there is a limited runway to how much it can continue buying back shares at 15% a year. My view is that DBX should trade like a business in permanent decline, and if we use the Gordon growth formula, where the variables are growth (-2%) and cost of equity (10%), it implies a multiple of 8x (0.98/0.12), and that is ~20% downside from here.

Risk

The main risk would be a faster stabilization in the core business and also early traction from new products. Management has highlighted encouraging retention gains from initiatives like redesigned cancellation workflows, which could slow or even reverse the decline in paying users. If this proves sustainable, it would directly challenge my bear case for continuous paying user decline. Also, stronger-than-expected adoption of Dash is still a potential swing factor. If it gains meaningful uptake and opens new upsell opportunities sooner than expected, revenue trends could improve faster than I expect.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my view on DBX remains a sell rating. The headline EPS growth masks the reality that revenue is shrinking, paying users are in decline, and monetization trends are deteriorating. Management’s decision to deprioritize FormSwift and push cheaper plans accelerates this weakness, while Dash is still too early to offset the core decay. At ~10x forward P/E, I see limited upside and believe the stock deserves to trade closer to 8x, implying downside risk from here.

data.title: PFFD's Portfolio Breakdown
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue our series with portfolio breakdowns. Today, we take a closer look at Global X U.S. Preferred ETF (NYSEARCA:PFFD) and its holdings.

Fund Overview

PFFD was launched on 09/11/2017. As of 7/31/2025, the fund has around $2.3 billion in assets under management. The number of holdings of the fund is 224. Its expense ratio is 0.23%. The 5-year total return on NAV is 0.92%, and the 5-year total return on market price is 2.46%. The average credit rating is close to BBB.

Portfolio breakdown

Baby bonds

Close to 19% of the PFFD's portfolio is in exchange-traded securities. We divide them into two groups:

> PAR

The average yield to worst for this group is 5.23%, and the average market price $25.83.

< PAR

The average yield to worst of those bonds that trade below par is 6.80%, and the average market price is $21.36.

Mandatory convertible securities

17% of the portfolio of the fund is mandatory convertible securities. Their average current yield is 6.20%. You can see them in the graph below:

Traditional convertible preferred stocks

Those stocks are close to 5% of the fund's portfolio. You can see them below in the chart:

Trust preferred securities

The trust preferred securities are only 1% of the portfolio. Their average yield to worst is 7.63%. You can see them with their credit ratings below:

Traditional preferred securities

We continue with the biggest part of the PFFD's portfolio - traditional preferred securities - fixed rate preferred stocks, fixed to floating, resettable stocks, and floor rate stocks.

-Fixed-rate preferred stocks

The fixed-rate preferred stocks are around 41% of the portfolio.

> PAR

Below are those that trade above par with their credit ratings and current yields:

Those that are callable:

and those that have call dates - with an average yield to call of 5.93%:

< PAR

And here are the fixed-rate preferred stocks that trade below par with their credit ratings:

The average current yield of this group is 6.37%, and the average credit rating is Baa3.

Fixed to floating

Our methodology for valuing floating-rate or variable-rate preferred stocks simplifies the calculation of expected returns by replacing these instruments with fixed-rate equivalents using the current swap rate. We assume that after the call date, this fixed rate will remain in effect until maturity. For the purposes of the analysis, we use a 40-year horizon and calculate the XIRR on this basis. In addition, for easier visualization in the charts, we also calculate the expected duration of this hypothetical fixed security. The fixed-to-floating securities represent 6% of the portfolio.

> PAR

The average weighted current yield of those that trade above par is 7.39%, the average weighted price is $25.46, and the average weighted credit rating is Baa3:

< PAR

Those that trade below par have an average weighted current yield is 5.87%, an average weighted price of $24.79, and an average weighted credit rating of Baa3:

Resettable stocks

Resettable stocks are 5% of the portfolio.

> PAR

Resettable stocks that trade above par average weighted current yield is 7.05%, and the average weighted future rate is 6.99%. The average weighted price is $26. The credit rating average-weighted score is Baa3.

< PAR

Resettable stocks that trade below par have a weighted current yield of 5.22%, and the average weighted future rate is 7.18%. The average weighted price is 24.07. The credit rating average-weighted score is Ba1.

Floor rate stocks

The stocks with floor rates represent 6% of the portfolio. Their average weighted current yield is 6.25%, average weighted future rate is 5.34%, and average weighted price is 21.62. The average weighted floor rate is 3.83%. The average credit rating is Baa3.

Also, we calculate the XIRR and duration for all groups, as you can see in the chart below:

Conclusion

PFFD offers a well-diversified portfolio of preferred securities with a focus on traditional fixed dividend stocks. The average credit rating is around BBB, indicating moderate credit risk. The fund also includes higher-yielding components such as baby bonds and convertible securities, which enhance returns, especially those trading below par. Thanks to its low expenses and broad exposure, PFFD is a suitable vehicle for investors seeking income with moderate risk from preferred shares.

data.title: A Detailed Look At The FX Market After The Powell Speech - Technical Levels
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Forex markets are going bonkers since Powell's dovish speech at the Jackson Hole conference. The US dollar is getting sold off aggressively as the market interpreted dovish signs for a cut in September, even in a relatively nuanced speech - markets didn't need much!

The Canadian dollar is the worst performer (still up against the USD), while the JPY, euro and Australian dollar are all up above 1% as we speak. Discover currency charts and levels by performing order, from best to worst.

All FX Majors 4H Charts with the immediate key levels in play

Some relief for the Yen - USD/JPY

The yen is the biggest winner from the speech. Usually, the Japanese currency appreciates from converging interest rates. One of the reasons why the yen had been performing so badly in the past period was because of the pushback in rate cut expectations.

The pair was rangebound but is now aggressively heading to the 146.00 Key support levels to watch for USD/JPY:

  • 150.00 Main Resistance
  • Pivot at High of May Range from 147.50 to 148 (acting as immediate resistance) and convergence with the 4H MA 50 and 200
  • 146.00 Main Support

AUD/USD - A sharp rebound from the prior week selloff

AUD/USD has formed a significant double bottom and is now moving above its 50-period MA, going towards the 200 MA (0.6512). Bulls are pushing the pair from the immediate support zone, levels just below:

Levels to watch for AUD/USD:

  • 0.6580 to 0.66 Main Resistance
  • 0.6550 Pivot Zone (acting as mid-term resistance)
  • 0.6512 4H MA 200 (immediate resistance
  • 0.6480 to 0.65 Immediate Support

The Euro, always loving US Dollar weakness

Things have changed significantly since our most recent report on EUR/USD, with buyers bringing the pair above August highs.

Levels to watch for EUR/USD:

  • Main resistance 1.18 at 2025 highs (1.1830)
  • 1.1750 Intermediate Resistance/Pivot
  • 1.1640 4H MA 200 acting as immediate support
  • 1.16 Current main Support

The Swissie regains some strength - USD/CHF

The Swiss franc had been one of the worst performing currencies since the July rebound in the greenback, but things are changing!

Levels to watch for USD/CHF:

  • 0.8050 Main Resistance
  • 0.80 Immediate Pivot
  • 0.79 Main Support
  • 2025 Lows 0.78730

The Pound is back on track - GBP/USD

GBP/USD has just invalidated a head and shoulders pattern mentioned in a previous analysis of the pair; now evolving in an upwards channel, it also looks like an inverted head and shoulders (showing further bullish potential) could be in play.

Levels to watch for GBP/USD:

  • Top of channel around 1.37 to 1.3730
  • 1.36 Main Resistance
  • 1.34 current Daily pivot (acted as Support)
  • 1.32 Main Support

USD/CAD right back towards the July range

USD/CAD was moving stubbornly higher but has reversed course in a flash since our Wednesday NA Markets analysis. The North American currency pair is moving below its 50-period MA and is almost back below the 1.38 handle.

Levels to watch for USD/CAD:

  • Immediate resistance at Aug Highs 1.38750
  • 1.38 Major Pivot (immediate Support)
  • 1.3740 Support

Safe trades as the weekend approaches!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VEQT:CA's Built-In Global Allocation Is A Buy
data.description:
The Vanguard All-Equity ETF Portfolio (TSX:VEQT:CA) is a promising all weather capital growth ETF that attempts to leverage exposure to US, Canadian, developing economies ex-US/Canada and emerging markets to capture pockets of growth in certain markets at any given point of time and also potentially smoothen the risk profile of the portfolio through diversification.

Methodology

The methodology is like a fund of funds approach. Strictly speaking, it holds passive ETFs focused on various markets so should be a fund of ETFs to be more accurate. The allocation between ETFs and timing of rebalances is discretionary tilting the strategy towards an actively managed one. However, the underlying ETFs are usually passive. Below is a snapshot of how the ETF allocation looks like today. The portfolio is heavily concentrated on US equities at ~46%. Canada accounts for another sizeable chunk of ~30%. That leaves less room for ex-US/Canada exposure. If we take out the developed markets exposure too, the only reasonable source of true diversification and potential alpha generation i.e. emerging markets are only 7% of the portfolio.

I have strong views on international allocation redundancies in today's global era where US firms themselves are spread across geographies and already include diversification benefits - ranging from tech giants like Microsoft to consumer brands like Coca Cola. That view has slightly changed now due to unprecedented geopolitical uncertainties. Tariffs, depending on where they end up may also see some amount of deviations from the usual high correlations across the globe. However, despite the current situation, I still believe diversification benefits are limited with correlations often returning to their equilibrium high state even if there are brief periods of deviations.

In terms of growth too, I feel developed economies or even emerging markets are reeling under their own set of problems, particularly in the current scenario. Looking out beyond the US tech sector is no longer necessary for generating alpha. Developed economies are seeing growth and hence equity market stagnation broadly. Emerging markets like India are facing valuation challenges. The political and policy risks in the broader emerging market basket is high. Currency fluctuations have not been favorable as well.

VEQT:CA's reasonably high exposure to the US assures one thing. The risk and return profile is not a drastically altered one from the US broad markets - say the S&P 500 or a total market index. Whatever diversification we see today indicates possibility of active exposure management leading to better drawdown management or growth, but those differentials will not likely pull the VEQT:CA portfolio and performance far from the US baseline. So, the alpha potential is limited and risks are broadly in line with the US markets. How much of the limited alpha and risk diversification is actually captured, is a function of the active calls made by the portfolio management. If significant deviations from the current allocations do happen, the profile of returns could also change drastically. My observations are based on current holdings and we should broadly expect the allocations to remain in line with the current philosophy. However, there is nothing in the methodology per se stopping VEQT:CA from overloading on ex-US markets in a prolonged period like say the lost decade, where ex-US portfolios were well uncorrelated and generated better growth as well.

And Other Details

The current allocations are total market bets. That, plus the close to 55% exposure outside US, where financials dominate, makes the overall portfolio totally balanced between tech and financials. This is an interesting mix that should add a flavor of cyclical resilience as markets rotate between themes and economic cycles, while retaining growth possibilities.

An offshoot of the lower tech and higher financials exposure is a decent dividend yield of ~1.4% - again balanced and reflects the portfolio characteristics. I checked the yield history to see whether historical changes in allocations altered the yield meaningfully. It does fluctuate between 1.25% to 2%, showing all weather reasonable yields, but never a yield oriented portfolio either.

VEQT:CA's structure of being a wrapper around Canadian domiciled Vanguard component ETFs removes US or international tax compliance hurdles. Low internal turnover at the ETF level also mean fewer taxable capital gains to pass on to investors. The flip side of the structure is an unrecoverable withholding taxes on dividends on US and international ETFs. There is no pass through of foreign tax credits either in taxable accounts. The net effect is tax convenient but not tax optimal, particularly because of the dividend taxes on international ETFs. However, that does not alter thesis for VEQT:CA on its own.

The expense ratio of 0.24% includes the expenses of the underlying ETFs and is reasonable for an active international offering. US domiciled international ETFs charge higher than this usually - EFA has an expense ratio of 0.32% for example.

Performance and Active Outcomes

To understand how the VEQT:CA performs, I have compared total returns against VUN:CA (the US total market ETF that is 45% of the portfolio) and SPY (a proxy for US broad market large caps). The total returns have spanned across many market cycles including the 2020 crash, ensuing rebound, 2022 downturn and the overall mega cap rally from there on (including a recent tariff sell-off). Through many stress tests and bull rallies, VEQT:CA shows consistent and structural underperformance against the US counterparts. That is expected because most of the growth in the US has been led by tech mega caps that do not find material place in VEQT:CA (~45% exposure).

How does VEQT:CA do in downturns? In a prolonged downturn period in 2022, VEQT:CA does reduce drawdowns better than SPY, and slightly better than the US total market index. The outperformance is reasonable. In the recent tariff sell-off where correlations were slightly disturbed globally, emerging and developed markets exposure helped reduce downsides meaningfully again. At a time when the US markets were down by ~17%, VEQT:CA was down by only ~13.5%.

Buy for the Active Diversification

Performance data does show that expectations of broadly tracking the US markets hold. At the same time, the alpha generation is healthy within the limited room to play in, in uncorrelated markets and also in US specific or global downturns. We have not seen a prolonged flat stagnating market or a last decade like uncorrelated regime in the period of analysis. Hence, how active VEQT:CA can get in those special circumstances is still not proven.

Even if it maintains roughly the same exposures as it does today, the diversification will ensure lower drawdowns and bring in reasonable diversification benefits. However, it may not show a different trajectory of its own though i.e. while US is bleeding, VEQT:CA is thriving is not expected unless evidence of more active management emerges.

The upside opportunity loss may seem quite high in comparison to drawdowns saved. But I will allow VEQT:CA that benefit of doubt because what we have seen is a bull rally of material proportions in the past 5 years overall. The underperformance is a result of that. VEQT:CA may continue to underperform as US tech keeps growing, but I still rate it a Buy on diversification that will be visible in other market conditions that what the recent past has shown.

data.title: Zillow Home Value Index: 'Real' Home Values Continue To Fall
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Zillow, the real estate listing and brokerage website, provides a wealth of publicly available real estate data. Among these, the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI) offers a seasonally adjusted measure of home values and market trends. Its "flagship" ZHVI is based on data for "all homes, middle tier, smoothed and seasonally adjusted cut," aiming to represent the typical home value rather than the median. Introduced in 2006, the ZHVI includes various segmentations by geography and housing type. Here, we focus on the flagship ZHVI, which reflects typical home values.

Home values fell for a fifth straight month in July, according to the Zillow Home Value Index. However, after adjusting for inflation, real home values dropped for a 15th consecutive month, hitting their lowest level since April 2021.

Last month's ZHVI came in at $368,581, down 0.12% from the previous month and up 0.29% from one year ago. However, after adjusting for inflation, the real figures are -0.39% month-over-month and -3.63% year-over-year.

Zillow Home Value Index Methodology

Zillow’s ZHVI is measured using monthly changes in its "Zestimates," its proprietary home valuation model. The index is meant to be timely, comprehensive, and transparent, published three weeks after the close of a given month. Many other home value indexes are published with a significant time lag - like the S&P at Core Logic Case Shiller Index and the FHFA House Price Index.

ZHVI is calculated with a few assumptions:

  • Monthly changes are calculated with a weighted mean
  • The average Zestimate within a range determines the index level
  • It can be interpreted as the market’s total appreciation for a given geography or cut

In addition, historical appreciation is based on actual historical housing stock versus a fixed basket of homes, and home improvements are considered in appreciation unrelated to market movements.

The first chart below shows the flagship ZHVI series - the “typical home value” since 2000, not adjusted for inflation. The current nominal value is 0.70% off its all-time high from February 2025.

Next, we take a look at the same index values but adjust for inflation using the CPI. As you can see, it’s a different picture. According to the ZHVI, today’s home values are below that of the (inflation-adjusted) housing peak in 2006 and are currently 12.13% off its all-time high from July 2022.

Here is an overlay of the nominal and real indexes for a comparative look.

Housing Prices (ZHVI) and Mortgage Rates

The following charts overlay mortgage rates with both nominal and real ZHVI, highlighting their relationship. Typically, rising interest rates reduce buyer affordability, decreasing demand for homes. This can lead to downward pressure on home prices. With that said, today’s housing market is following a different path because we’re seeing elevated mortgage rates in conjunction with elevated home prices.

Zillow Home Value Index vs. S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Index

Zillow claims that the ZHVI are "the most accurate and timely measures of residential real estate prices in the United States" and that its index is more representative of housing stock than the S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller Home Price Index. Zillow claims this due to its extensive database that includes input from real homeowners as well as their sophisticated home valuation model in the Zestimate.

Zillow believes its ZHVI has distinct advantages over the Case-Shiller index. The Case-Shiller (CS) Index uses the repeat-sales methodology, meaning it measures price changes by looking at homes that have resold in a region, while the ZHVI only includes homes that have sold at least twice in recent history and excludes all new construction. Here are a few charts pulled directly from a piece by Zillow comparing the two indexes. We tried to get this data to do our own analysis, but the research group there would not provide it. Here’s a link to that article.

Another difference between the CS and ZHVI is that CS includes foreclosure resales. Zillow emphasizes that foreclosure resales are "substantially different from non-distressed sales" and are often considerably discounted from such. Zillow claims that using foreclosure resales represents "two very different market segments." Eric Stubbs, Ph.D. at RBC WM, believes foreclosure sales is a matter of taste and says that foreclosures are actual sales, so they do in fact affect the price of surrounding properties.

In any case, the ZHVI is another tool in the tool belt to determine home values and is useful due to its timeliness and variety of information.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include: iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Banco BBVA Argentina: Banking In The Eye Of The Storm
data.description:
Investment Thesis

BBVA Argentina (NYSE:BBAR) is at a complex breakeven point: on the one hand, the results of Q2 2025 confirm that the local operation remains solid, with revenues growing above inflation, efficiency defended and regulatory capital remaining loose. On the other hand, the macroeconomic environment changed abruptly: the elimination of LEFI in mid-July erased a key liquidity tool for banks, forcing them to rethink their foundation and risk exposure structure.

In my previous article I highlighted the resilience of the bank against an adverse scenario, supported by its ability to translate inflation into prices and a solid balance sheet. Today that resilience remains, but the market no longer rewards it in the same way: the stock corrected strongly in recent weeks, reflecting both the political uncertainty of legislative elections and the lack of a clear monetary anchor.

The current multiples (P/E below 5x, P/B around 0.5x) show BBVA Argentina as a visibly cheap stock vis-à-vis banks in the region. However, this low price is more a reflection of Argentina’s systemic risk than an unequivocal signal of opportunity.

Therefore, my position moves from a constructive view with limited upside to a prudent hold: the value is there, but the convergence driver depends on monetary policy and the election result.

Overview

BBVA Argentina (BBAR) maintains a relevant position within the local financial system, with a double-digit market share in deposits and loans, a consolidated network of retail and corporate clients, and the strategic backing of its parent company in Spain. The local operation continues to function as a key pillar for the group, but the dynamic is completely conditioned by the Argentine macro.

The H1 2025 was marked by a regulatory shock: the elimination of LEFI in July, which forced the Central Bank to redesign its monetary absorption scheme. This changed the rules of the game for banks, which no longer have a safe and liquid investment instrument, increasing their exposure to balance sheet risk.

At the same time, political uncertainty in the run-up to the legislative elections keeps FX volatility high, with the market watching to see if the government can sustain the positive real rates scheme without overshooting dollar demand.

In this context, BBAR continues to show operational strength: interest income growing with vigor, commissions providing diversification and efficiency defended in an inflationary scenario. The credit portfolio is still growing at a moderate pace, but BBAR maintains strong asset quality and a low regulatory capital ratio.

Q2 2025

In Q2 2025, BBVA Argentina presented a sharp decline compared to Q2 2024. Net earnings adjusted for inflation decreased by 62.1%YoY, to AR$60B (vs AR$157B), and in H1 2025, the contraction was also marked: net profit was AR$146B (-31.7%YoY).

Profitability also deteriorated. During H1 2025, ROA reduced to 1.5%, from 3.0% in 2024, while the ROE fell to 9.6%, compared with 13.3% in Q2 2024.

In terms of financial margin, the total NIM keep on constant at 19.1%, while disaggregate shows a difference: the NIM in pesos was 21.7%, just below the 21.8% of Q2 2025, but the NIM in dollars presented a strong recovery, expanding to 5.4% from the 3.9% in 2024.

On the activity side, loans to the consolidated private sector was AR$11.3T, with a real growth of 109.6% YoT. This increase was driven mainly by current account advances, pre-financing and export finance, and commercial loans. Market share in private finance advanced 107pb YoY, to 11.61%.

In deposits, BBAR also showed strength. Consolidated stock reached AR$13T, growing 64% YoY in real terms. This performance responded to the dynamism of fixed maturities, which increased by more than 36% during the year, and to savings accounts, especially in foreign currency. As a result, the market share in private deposits rose 214 bps YoY to 9.64%.

Portfolio quality remained relatively under control: late payments closed at 2.28%, with a coverage level of 115.15%. In efficiency, the ratio was 56.5%, practically unchanged from last year.

Finally, related to solvency and liquidity, BBAR showed strength. The regulatory capital ratio (Tier1) was 18.4%, presenting an overintegration of 123.9% exceeding the minimum required. Liquid assets accounted for 48.7% of deposits, confirming BBAR’s solid defensive position.

Valuation, Growth and Profitability

The picture that emerges from the current multiples of BBAR clearly shows a typical paradox of Argentine banks: extremely depressed prices in the market against metrics of profitability and efficiency which, in relative terms, appear far above their global peers.

In terms of valuation, BBAR is traded with a P/E TTM of just 2.38x, well below the regional and global average, reflecting the high country risk premium and investors' distrust of the sustainability of the local financial cycle. Even when the P/E FWD (2.96x) is observed, the company continues at levels that, under a standardized framework, would be typical of a crisis scenario, not of stability.

Something similar happens with EV/EBITDA (1.11x) and P/B (0.71x): both suggest a valuation of liquidation rather than growth, even though the bank has been showing expansion in its portfolio and profitability improvements.The market, ultimately, is not recognizing the bank’s ability to generate value under an inflationary environment and positive real rates. Punishment is directly associated with political uncertainty, macroeconomic fragility and the history of financial shocks in Argentina.

As we move to growth, metrics are equally telling. The Revenue YoY expanded 276%, which reflects the nominality of the Argentine economy, but also the ability of the bank to transfer inflationary dynamics to its income structure. The YoY EBITDA grew 278% and the YoY Net Income rose 152%, confirming that the institution not only keeps pace with inflation but also manages to sustain margins in an adverse context.

Margins make the difference: Gross Profit Margin is 82%, EBIT Margin is 57% and Net Income Margin is 13.5%. These levels would be unthinkable in developed market banks, and yet they are supported by BBAR thanks to an operating model that relies on broad spreads and contained costs.

As for profitability, the indicators complete the picture: ROE at 31.7% and ROA at 4.2%. In both cases, levels that any global bank would envy, although here they collide again with the perception of systemic risk that dominates the multiples.

In summary, the valuation, growth and profitability figures suggest that BBAR remains a deeply undervalued stock. However, the reading cannot be simplistic, since the price consistently includes political and macroeconomic uncertainty. The upside potential exists, but it is tied to Argentina’s ability to stabilize its economic cycle and reduce country risk.

Risks

The main risk of investing in BBAR does not lie in the intrinsic quality of the bank, but in the context in which it operates. The Argentine financial system operates with a level of savings dollarization that limits its capacity for domestic anchoring, and with a history of shocks including sovereign defaults, capital controls and abrupt regulatory changes. This implies that even when balance sheets appear solid, the fragility of the environment can quickly alter expectations.

One of the most relevant risks is exposure to local macroeconomics. An acceleration of inflation, a change in monetary policy or the reintroduction of stricter controls on capital movements would have a direct impact on operations. The bank’s ability to sustain margins depends largely on maintaining the current positive real rate pattern, which is not guaranteed in a political volatility scenario.

Another obvious risk is regulatory risk. In Argentina, history shows that the financial sector is often used as a tool of economic policy, whether through forced encroachments, dividend restrictions or rate caps. For a bank like BBAR, these restrictions create tensions in the allocation of capital.

Finally, the political risk can't be ignored. Economic reforms and stabilization are still underway, and the next legislative elections in October 2025 add additional uncertainty. The momentum seems to indicate that the market is already anticipating the possibility that political support for reforms will lose strength, which increases the perception of risk and puts pressure on all Argentine assets, including banking assets.

Conclusion

The BBAR numbers are strong: valuation at auction levels, nominal growth well above inflation and profit margins that in any other market would justify much higher multiples. However, the international market continues to punish action, and rightly so: systemic and political risks outweigh individual metrics.

This leaves BBAR in an ambivalent situation. On the one hand, it offers a huge upside potential if Argentina manages to stabilize its economy and reduce country risk, since the multiples could converge towards more reasonable levels. On the other hand, the risk of reversal is permanent: a macro shock, a regulatory intervention or a political crisis can wipe out much of that market value in a matter of days.

This is why I consider that the most prudent position, then, is to maintain a "strategic hold". For investors with high risk tolerance, I think BBAR can be seen as a tactical option to capture upside in the event of an improved local environment, while for a more conservative portfolio it is a position that requires caution and constant monitoring.

data.title: The Appeal Of Small-Cap Stocks
data.description:
Since March 2021, on a total return basis, small-cap stocks have underperformed large-cap stocks by almost 60%, according to Bloomberg. While surging prices for tech stocks (e.g., the Fab 7) go a long way to explaining why this has happened, it's also likely that tight monetary policy in recent years has also boosted large cap stocks at the expense of small caps.

This case was bolstered by today's price action, which followed Chairman Powell's speech in Jackson Hole, in which he indicated the Fed is getting ready to resume the easing of monetary policy that started two years ago but has stalled since then.

Today was a good day for all stocks, but small-cap stocks advanced 2.3% more than large-cap stocks. So far this month, the total return on small-cap stocks has exceeded the return on the S&P 500 by 4.7%. The following charts are telling us that this dynamic could have a lot more room to run.

Chart #1

Chart #1 compares the level of the S&P 500, the most popular and well-regarded index of large-cap stock prices, with the Russell 2000, the equivalent index for small-cap stocks.

Over time, the chart shows that these two indices have experienced gains of similar magnitude, but there are times when they diverge. The two y-axis values are based on the fact that the S&P 500 index value tends to be about double that of the Russell 2000 index; or conversely, the Russell value tends to be about half that of the S&P 500.

Background info: The Russell 2000 index contains the smallest 2000 stocks of the Russell 3000 index, and has a current market cap of about $3.1 trillion. The S&P 500 index, in contrast, has a market cap of about $55 trillion, and is composed of what could arguably be termed the 500 largest and most successful companies in the U.S.

It's hard to imagine two indices with greater differences: big and successful vs. very small and obscure - David vs. Goliath. Yet there are times when small caps outperform large caps, and we could be at the beginning of one of those times.

Chart #2

Chart #2 shows the ratio of the Russell 2000 index to the S&P 500 index. Small-cap stocks have underperformed large caps by roughly 40% over the past 12 years, with the ratio declining from 0.64 to 0.36. Over the period shown, the ratio has averaged 0.5, and it appears to be mean-reverting. Should the ratio revert to 0.5, that would imply a small-cap outperformance of roughly 35%.

Chart #3

Chart #3 compares the ratio of the small cap/S&P 500 indices to the level of the yield on 5-yr TIPS, which in turn is a decent proxy for how tight Fed monetary policy is (it's inverted to show that a rising red line equates to easier monetary policy, and a falling red line equates to tighter monetary policy).

With the exception of a few periods, these two lines tend to track each other, thus reinforcing the thesis that monetary policy has an important influence on the relative behavior of small- and large-cap stocks.

It is commonly thought that easy money is good for small-cap stocks because small companies are generally more leveraged and thus more sensitive to changes in interest rates, whereas large companies are less leveraged and thus better able to weather adverse conditions, among other things.

What stands out of late is the divergence of the two lines, which began about two years ago. Monetary policy has been slowly easing (as seen in the rising red line) but the small-cap ratio has been declining.

Today's price action - in which small caps outperformed large caps by 2.3% - marks what could be an important turning point in favor of small caps - thus reestablishing historic patterns. Note that a similar (but opposite) divergence happened in the 2005-2008 period.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
